_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Talbot County, MD. Records survival is generally good — no catastrophic courthouse fire, no military destruction. The county's records from 1662 onward are substantially complete, making Talbot one of the richest documentary sources on the Eastern Shore. Frederick Douglass's enslavement and the Lloyd family plantation records add extraordinary depth to the county's archival holdings.

current_courthouse.location: Easton, MD — 11 N. Washington Street (expanded and renovated multiple times; one of the oldest courthouses in continuous use in Maryland)
records_housed: wills and estates — some early records transferred to Maryland State Archives

previous_courthouses.location: York (original county seat, now Royal Oak area). Courts met in private homes and taverns before a dedicated courthouse was built.
previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Talbot Court House (later Easton) by 1710. Early records transported.

previous_courthouses.location: Talbot Court House (Easton) — frame courthouse on the courthouse green.
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current brick courthouse in 1794. Records survived the transition.

record_losses.cause: Incomplete record-keeping in the earliest decades; some early court records fragmentary
records_destroyed: some court minutes from the 1660s-1670s are incomplete
records_survived: land records from 1662 onward substantially complete
court proceedings from the 1680s onward are detailed and continuous
record_losses.description: Talbot County's record losses are minor. The county never suffered a major courthouse fire or military destruction. The earliest records (1660s-1670s) are fragmentary, but from the 1680s onward the documentary record is rich and continuous. The county's prosperity ensured that records were maintained, bound, and preserved with care.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was in Talbot County after 1680, the records are likely to survive — land deeds, court proceedings, probate inventories, and tax lists form a continuous chain.
record_losses.source: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.

deeds.completeness: Very good. Land records from 1662 forward. Provincial patent books at the Maryland State Archives supplement the county records for the earliest period.
deeds.gaps: Minor gaps in the 1660s-1670s. Substantially complete from 1680 onward.
deeds.repository: Maryland State Archives; Talbot County Circuit Court, Easton

wills_estates.completeness: Very good. Wills, inventories, and estate administrations from 1665. The colonial-era inventories are detailed and genealogically valuable — they list personal property including enslaved individuals by name in some cases.
wills_estates.gaps: Minor fragmentary gaps in the earliest years.
wills_estates.repository: Maryland State Archives; Talbot County Register of Wills

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1794. Earlier marriages documented in church records (Anglican vestry books, Quaker meeting records).
marriages.gaps: No civil marriage records before 1794. Third Haven Quaker Meeting records from the 1660s and Christ Church (Anglican) records from the early 1700s provide substantial substitute coverage.
marriages.repository: Maryland State Archives; Talbot County Clerk of Court

court_minutes.completeness: Good. Court proceedings from 1662 with minor gaps in the earliest decades. From the 1680s onward, court records are detailed and continuous.
court_minutes.online_access: Maryland State Archives; Archives of Maryland Online

vital_records.completeness: Maryland statewide registration began 1898. Compliance was incomplete until the 1910s.
vital_records.gaps: No civil vital records before 1898. Church records, cemetery records, and newspaper notices are the substitutes.
vital_records.repository: Maryland Division of Vital Records (post-1898); Maryland State Archives (earlier substitutes)

substitute_sources.repository: Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College; Maryland State Archives
substitute_sources.description: Third Haven Monthly Meeting near Easton is one of the oldest Quaker meetings in America. Birth, marriage, death, and disciplinary records from 1661 onward. Exceptionally well-maintained. The Quaker records predate the county's own records.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Exceptional. For Quaker families on the Eastern Shore, Third Haven records are the primary genealogical source. The meeting's discipline records also document disownments (often for marrying outside the faith or for holding enslaved people), which provide narrative detail.

substitute_sources.repository: Maryland Diocesan Archives; Maryland State Archives
substitute_sources.description: Vestry books, baptism and burial registers for the established Anglican parish in Easton.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Very good for Anglican families. Vestry books document civil functions (poor relief, indentures) as well as church membership.

substitute_sources.source_type: Frederick Douglass Papers and Lloyd Family Papers
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Congress; Maryland Historical Society; Talbot County Free Library
substitute_sources.description: The Lloyd family papers document one of the largest plantation operations on the Eastern Shore, including enslaved individuals. Frederick Douglass's autobiographies provide firsthand documentation of slavery in Talbot County.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for enslaved families connected to the Lloyd plantation (Wye House). Douglass's writings name individuals and describe family relationships that appear nowhere in the plantation records.

substitute_sources.description: Labor contracts, marriage registrations, and complaints filed with the Freedmen's Bureau office serving Talbot County. These records document the transition from slavery to freedom.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for Black families. Marriage registrations often name parents and former enslavers. Labor contracts document employer-employee relationships that trace to former enslaver-enslaved relationships.

narrative_hooks: Talbot County's records survived — no fire, no flood, no army — and if {ancestor_name} was in the county after 1680, the paper trail is likely intact: deeds, wills, court minutes, and the tax lists that counted everyone who owned or owed.

The Third Haven Quaker records begin in 1661 — a year before the county itself was created — and if {ancestor_name} was a Friend, the meeting kept records of births, marriages, deaths, and moral failings with a precision that put the county government to shame.

Frederick Douglass wrote his way out of Talbot County — and his words document the enslaved community with a specificity that the plantation ledgers never achieved, naming people by their names and their suffering.

The Freedmen's Bureau in Talbot County recorded what the slavery system had refused to record — marriages, family connections, and the names of parents — because the people who had been property were now persons in the record.
source_references: Maryland State Archives. Guide to Government Records. Annapolis, 2005.
Douglass, Frederick. Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass. Boston: Anti-Slavery Office, 1845.
Friends Historical Library. Third Haven Meeting Records Guide. Swarthmore College.
National Archives. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands (Record Group 105).
